title: Chicken jalfrezi
subtitle: Sharp, hot stir-fried chicken curry with peppers and tomato
excerpt: Chicken pieces stir-fried with sliced onion, green and red peppers, fresh green chilli, and tomatoes in a dry, fragrant sauce. One of the fastest and brightest curry-house dishes: cooked quickly over high heat, the vegetables stay slightly crisp and the sauce is dry rather than saucy. Bold, sharp, and hot.
sourceNotes: Jalfrezi (from Urdu jalfrezi, 'hot fry') is a dry-fry curry popular in the British-Bangladeshi curry-house tradition. It is listed in British curry-house menus from the 1980s onward and is widely considered one of the most popular dishes on the standard British curry-house menu.

content.text: Jalfrezi is a high-heat dish and that is what sets it apart from the slow-cooked curries. The wok or wide pan must be very hot before the chicken goes in, and the vegetables are added late so they stay fresh and slightly crisp. The sauce is dry: there is no water added and the tomatoes cook down quickly into a coating rather than a gravy. The result has more in common with a stir-fry than a braise.

Fresh green chillies are important here. They give a different kind of heat from chilli powder: brighter, more volatile, and more aromatic. Add them with the peppers so they cook briefly but retain their freshness. For a milder version, deseed the green chillies before adding; for full heat, leave the seeds in.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wok or wide pan over maximum heat. Add the {{chicken-thighs-boneless}} pieces in a single layer and cook without moving for 3 minutes until seared. Stir and cook for another 2 minutes. Remove and set aside. Add the {{onion}} to the pan and stir-fry for 5 minutes until softened and starting to colour. Add the {{garlic}} and {{ginger-root}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, {{turmeric}}, and {{chilli-powder}} and stir for 30 seconds.

Add the {{pepper-green}}, {{pepper-red}}, and {{chilli-green}} and stir-fry for 3 minutes. Add the {{tomato}} and cook for 4 minutes until they break down into a thick coating. Return the chicken to the pan, season with salt, and cook for 5 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the sauce clings to everything. Stir in the {{garam-masala}}, scatter over fresh coriander if using, and serve immediately with rice or naan.

items.fix: Keep the heat high throughout. Do not cover the pan. The tomatoes need to cook down quickly rather than steam
items.cause: Pan not hot enough or lid placed on during cooking

items.fix: Sear for 3 minutes only, the chicken will finish cooking when returned to the sauce. Thighs are more forgiving than breast
Add the peppers only after the onion and spices are cooked. They need only 3 minutes over high heat to soften while retaining some texture
items.cause: Added too early or cooked too long

items.fix: The green chillies are the primary heat source in jalfrezi. Leave the seeds in for proper heat, or add extra chilli powder
items.cause: Green chillies removed or seeds discarded

content.text: Jalfrezi sits in the middle of the British curry-house heat spectrum, hotter than korma or passanda but milder than madras or vindaloo. Its distinguishing character is the texture: the sliced peppers and onions give it a freshness and crunch that the long-braised curries don't have. It is a quick weeknight dish, faster than rogan josh or dhansak by 45 minutes or more. At the height of the British curry-house era in the 1990s, jalfrezi competed with tikka masala as the most ordered dish on the menu.
